title: Cecilia L. Ridgeway: Framed by Gender: How Gender Inequality Persists in the Modern World
abstract: The 20th century was marked by the rise and worldwide diffusion of liberal egalitarian social movements, cultural ideals, and bureaucratic forms. Principles of equal opportunity and non-discrimination today garner near-universal affirmation in national and international forums, and women’s free access to labour markets, educational systems, and polities is commonly regarded as a signifier of modernity and social progress. Much progress towards gender equality has indeed been made, with women almost everywhere gaining formal legal equality (e.g. voting rights) and greatly increasing their representation in major public-sphere institutions. But within these institutions—as within families—strong gender differentiation persists. And contrary to conventional modernization accounts, some forms of gender inequality are even more pronounced in affluent reputably gender-progressive societies than in poorer more traditional ones. Why is gender inequality so resilient even in the face of powerful cultural and structural forces towards equality? This is the question at the heart of Cecilia Ridgeway’s new book, Framed by Gender: Why Gender Inequality Persists in the Modern World. By deftly integrating a multilevel theoretical framework with decades of interdisciplinary research, Ridgeway constructs a compelling general account of gender’s persisting force in shaping our perceptions, behaviours, institutions, and opportunities. The book’s main argument is that the persistence of gender inequality is attributable to the ongoing primacy of gender as a frame for organizing social relations and institutions. Gender is one of the fundamental axes of social identity in the contemporary United States (along with race and age), and people rely on widely shared gender stereotypes to coordinate their work tasks and to reduce uncertainty in organizing social encounters. To buttress her arguments, Ridgeway discusses evidence from decades of research on gender beliefs and status hierarchies, including from her own experimental work that builds on the ‘expectations states’ theoretical tradition.
